course_description: Examination of basic principles of income tax law, including analysis of concepts of income, assessable income, allowable deductions & capital gains & calculation of tax payable - with some focus on different tax structures.

requirements.restrictions: Not to be studied by LLB or LLB dual students Quota: Summer semester minimum of 30 enrolments
requirements.incompatible: CO324 or 821 or LA413 or 444 or QB238 or COMM3206 or 7203 or LAWS5144 or 7012 or 7144 or 7344
requirements.assumed_background: You are assumed to have studied and passed LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent) and to have retained from that course an understanding of the Australian legal system and the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning in the analysis and resolution of legal problems. It is also assumed that students will have a working knowledge of the accounting principles of income and expenditure. An understanding of how depreciation is calculated in accounting terms is also useful.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

The overall aim of the course is to provide students with a comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of income tax in Australia and how these principles are to be applied to individuals. It should be noted that it is not an objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affect the actual preparation of income tax returns.

learning_outcomes.description: Prove a knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.
Demonstrate an understanding of the income tax formula and the elements which are the basis of it and be able to apply those elements to the case of individual taxpayers.

Interpret provisions of the relevant Income Tax Assessment Act and apply them by preparing a concise written argument sufficient to form the basis of sound advice to a taxpayer and/or calculating the financial implications of a particular transaction.
Critically evaluate case law and Rulings & Determinations issued by the Commissioner of Taxation.

Solve legal problems/issues relating to assessable income; deductions; capital gains tax; tax offsets; tax payable; Fringe Benefits Tax and the Goods & Services Tax; taxation administration; and current issues in taxation.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of taxation law.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.

assessment_details.title: 7012 Assignment
assessment_details.task_description:
The assignment will contain 2 questions and is worth 30% of the asessment in this course.

The assignment will cover material from Topics 1 to 4 and involves research into various tax issues covered by these topics.

More details on the assignment, including detailed marking criteria will be provided during the first lecture.

The assignment should be approximately 1500 words in length.

The assignment must be submitted using the relevant LAW Assignment Cover Sheet to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre, Level 1, Colin Clark Building (39).

A copy of the assignment must also be submitted electronically via Blackboard.

The assignment will be released on Friday 9 December at 9:00am .

The assignment will be due on Friday 13 January before 4:00pm.

Submission:

The Assignment must be submitted using the relevant LAWS Assignment Cover Sheet to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre, Level 1, Colin Clark Building (39). The assignment must also be submitted electronically via the 'Online Submission of Assignment' folder on the course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description:
The final exam will cover material from all eight topics. The questions will be problem style questions.

Students may take any printed materials into the exam, including all of the prescribed textbooks (which may be annotated). Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices will be allowed. Students will also need a University approved calculator.

Taxation texts other than those prescribed for this course will not be permitted into the final exam.

learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (3rd edition) .

The Law Library has produced a How-to Guide to assist students to follow the AGLC3 citation style.

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://www.library.uq.edu.au/law/lawsites.phtml provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Blackboard Website

Where the course utilises Blackboard , the Blackboard website will contain: course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides; discussion boards; and intra-course email facilities. Students should visit the course Blackboard website regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

The Course Coordinator will maintain and regularly update the course Blackboard website, which can be accessed via the following link http://blackboard.elearn.uq.edu.au . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the website . Access to Blackboard is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the commencement of classes. If you do not have access in the first week of classes please contact the Law School Webmaster by email webmaster@law.uq.edu.au .

AskIT , available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT desk at Level 2 of the Duhig Building at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich - http://askit.uq.edu.au .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The course website will contain all necessary resources and information not included in the prescribed texts.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Mon 10:00 - Mon 12:00 Topic 1: Taxation System in Australia : An introduction to Australia's taxation system. We also examine the basic structure of the tax legislation. We conclude with an examination of the impact of residency on taxation within Australia. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 4 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 1); ATO Homepage; PoTL; ATLC; PoTL [2.480]; Legislation Mon 13:00 - Mon 15:00 Topic 2: Income | Lecture 1 : This topic will consider the nature of income and its relevance to the taxation equation. In particular the meaning of assessable income and its distinction from capital receipts. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation Tue 10:00 - Tue 12:00 Topic 2: Income | Lecture 2 : This topic will examine business income and how certain business transactions may generate assessable income. We will review certain types of income specifically included in assessable income in accordance with the Income Tax Assessment Act. We also review the concept of exempt income. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation Tue 13:00 - Tue 15:00 Topic 3: Income Tax Accounting : This topic will examine when income is derived (or earned) thereby becoming assessable income. Similarily, we examine when deductions are incurred (or expensed). We examine the concept of Cash v Accrual accounting from a taxation perspective. Also included is the nature and valuation of trading stock for income tax purposes. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation Wed 10:00 - Wed 12:00 Topic 4: Capital Gains Tax - CGT | Lecture 1 : This first lecture will introduce the concepts of CGT. An examination of exemptions and concessions available to taxpayers, including business entities is also included. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL; Legislation Wed 13:00 - Wed 15:00 Topic 4: Capital Gains Tax - CGT | Lecture 2 : This second lecture examines how to calculate capital gains and losses. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL; Legislation Fri 10:00 - Fri 12:00 Topic 5: Deductions | Lecture 1 : In the first lecture we will consider how certain expenditures can be used to reduce assessable income. The importance of deductions in the taxation equation is examined. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation Fri 13:00 - Fri 15:00 Topic 5: Deductions | Lecture 2 : In the second lecture we will consider how certain expenditures are specifically allowed or disallowed under the Income Tax Assessment Act. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Thu 10:00 - Thu 12:00 Review of Tutorial Problems - Topics 1 & 2 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topics 1 & 2); PoTL Thu 13:00 - Thu 15:00 Review of Tutorial Problems - Topics 3 & 4 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topics 3 & 4); PoTL

Fri 9:00 Assignment Released (Assignment) : The LAWS3101 assignment will be released on Friday, 9 December 2011. Student should conduct research for their assignment. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 6 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website (Assessment Folder); ATO Homepage; PoTL [2.480]

Mon 10:00 - Mon 12:00 Topic 5: Deductions | Lecture 3 : The final lecture in the Deductions series examines the concept of depreciation (capital allowances) from a taxation perspective and how deductions relating to motor vehicle expenses are calculated in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Assessment Act. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL; Legislation Mon 13:00 - Mon 15:00 Topic 6: The Tax Assessment : This topic brings together all elements of the Tax Formula. We examine how to calculate tax payable on taxable income. We review the issuing of a tax assessment and some of the administrative practices of the ATO. Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL; Legislation Tue 10:00 - Tue 12:00 Topic 7: Employers & Employees : This topic examines the taxation issues associated with employing staff. In particular we examine Superannuation Guarantee, PAYG and Fringe Benefit Tax issues. Learning Objectives: 1, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 7); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation Tue 13:00 - Tue 15:00 Topic 8: Indirect Taxation in Australia : This topic will consider Australia's indirect taxation regime, in particular the Goods & Services Tax (GST). Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 8); PoTL; Legislation Fri 10:00 - Fri 12:00 Revision Lecture : Review of Topics 1-8. Details of final exam to be provided. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topics 1-8)

Wed 10:00 - Wed 12:00 Review of Tutorial Problems - Topic 5 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial tasks posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL Wed 13:00 - Wed 15:00 Review of Tutorial Problems - Topics 6,7 & 8 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial tasks posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topics 6,7 & 8); PoTL

Fri 16:00 Assignment Due (Assignment) : The LAWS3101 assignment is due at 4:00pm on Friday, 13 January 2012. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 6 Readings/Ref: ATO Database; Course Website; ATO Homepage; PoTL [2.480]

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

A Student Feedback document will be available for both the 7012 Assignment and the Final Exam.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the Schools policy ( http://www.law.uq.edu.au/obtaining-feedback-on-final-examinations).

Special Examinations

Centrally Controlled Special Examinations

Students should refer to myAdvisor for the policy relating to centrally controlled special examinations - http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910

School Special Examinations

Where a student does not attend a mid-semester examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

Where, due to a medical condition occurring on the day of the examination, a student is unable to attend a mid-semester examination, or the performance of a student in the examination has been impaired, the student may, on the presentation of a certificate covering the date of the examination from a registered medical practitioner and a written explanation of how the medical condition prevented attendance/impaired performance at the examination, be permitted to sit a special examination in lieu.

For full details, students should refer to the School policy on Special Mid-Semester Examinations .

Applications for a special mid-semester examination shall be made by lodging the Application for Special Mid-Semester Examination form (and supporting documentation) with the Law School Office within 5 days following the date of the mid-semester examination.

Special mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester special examinations and may take the format of an oral examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25123 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Income Tax Law is a complex and continually changing area of law. This course is designed to provide a broad understanding of the major topics which affect taxpayers generally. The emphasis throughout will be on practical concepts with appropriate weight being given to the research of both legal questions and recent developments in the law.

The overall aim of the course is to provide students with a comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of taxation in Australia and how these principles are to be applied to individuals. It should be noted that it is not an objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affect the actual preparation of income tax returns.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Student feedback did not warrant any changes to the course.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic,

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned,

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

In a negative sense, lectures are not for:

Delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn,

Reading material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of the Tutorial Series

A Tutorial Clinic program has been established to provide students with an open forum to review their answers to Tutorial Problems and to ask questions.

Detailed answers to the Tutorial Problems will not be provided but a copy of all material used in the Clinics will be posted to the Course Blackboard site during the week following the relevant Clinic session.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

1% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (including essays, assignments, take-home tests, etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day (or part thereof) that the assessment is overdue.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date will receive zero marks. For the purposes of clarification, if the due date is the 10th, any work submitted on the 16th or later will receive zero marks. If the 6th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, assessment will be accepted until 9am on the following Monday.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Law School's policy on Late Submission of Assessment . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension form (and supporting documentation) at the Law School Office by the due date for assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.

Students who are granted an extension beyond the due date for assessment items must submit their assessment to the Law School Office, NOT the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre.
other_assessment_information: All assignments are now to be electronically submitted via Blackboard. The instructions for submission are in the Online Submission Folder in your course Blackboard site. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this ECP.
